FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline cohort aged 9-10 years, 9254 participants enrolled

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional study using latent factors and logistic mixed models; reports associations between SMA and recreational activity engagement

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in BMC Public Health

FT_IC2.evidence: The other side of the relationship is recreational activity endorsement (sport, music, art hobbies). These are behavioral activity participations, not individual-trait-level variables (which require neural, mental health, cognitive, well-being, personality, substance use, sleep, academic, or similar within-person constructs). Recreational activity endorsement alone is contextual/activity participation, not an individual trait.

notes: Paper fails on FT-IC2 because recreational activity endorsement (participation in sports, music, art, hobbies) is not an individual-trait-level outcome. These are behavioral participations, not within-person constructs like mental health or cognitive function.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study (Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development Study), baseline age 9-10 years, 9986 families

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: network psychometric analysis using mixed graphical models examining associations between screen time types and mood symptoms with BIS/BAS and parental monitoring controls

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology

FT_IC2.evidence: Age-inappropriate screen time (separate measurement from other screen time types) paired as IV with mood symptoms (depressive mood, anhedonia, irritability, elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, anger) as individual-trait-level mental health outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Network analysis reports standardized associations; age-inappropriate screen time was 'stably and significantly associated with various elevated mood symptoms, independent from other types of screen time, BIS/BAS, and parental monitoring'; permutation test results and network edge weights reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported including network edge weights, node centrality measures, and symptom connectivity metrics

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 only applies to longitudinal designs
notes: Meets all inclusion criteria. Cross-sectional network analysis of screen time and mood symptoms with appropriate control variables.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study uses 'Dutch ABCD Study and the Spanish INMA Project' — not the U.S. ABCD Study. The methods section explicitly states 'children of 9-11 years old (n=2,952) and adolescents of 17-18 years old (n=261), from the Dutch ABCD Study and the Spanish INMA Project'

notes: Excluded on FT-IC1: uses Dutch ABCD (European cohort) and Spanish INMA Project, not the U.S. ABCD Study required for inclusion.
